text: The surgeon makes a skin incision approximately three millimeters long along the anterior axillary line, located two centimeters above the iliac crest.

The surgeon uses a disposable cannula to infiltrate the subcutaneous tissue with a modified solution, injecting approximately 120 milliliters on each side, adjusting for the amount of adipose tissue in the anterior abdominal wall.
For example in case of tiny patient it is enough generally one hundred and twenty but sometimes in case of overweight even if mild it is necessary to infiltrate one hundred and eighty each side.

After a fifteen-minute wait, the surgeon performs liposuction using a 13-gauge cannula connected to a vac-lock syringe, extracting subcutaneous adipose tissue from both sides of the anterior abdominal wall.
The aspirated tissue is transferred to a 10-milliliter syringe and then placed into a cylinder containing saline solution and metallic spheres.
The cylinder, equipped with blue and grey filters at its edges, is shaken until the saline solution inside becomes clear.
Steel spheres within the cylinder emulsify and microfracture the adipose tissue, visibly breaking it down into smaller fragments.
The final product flows through the gray filter into the connected syringe, completing the transfer process.
The processed product is transferred into multiple one-milliliter syringes, each equipped with a twenty-two gauge needle, in preparation for injection.
A 3D transanal ultrasound image displays the anal sphincter gap, visible from the nine to twelve o'clock position.
The surgeon uses their fingers to palpate and assess the anal sphincter gap, identifying and localizing the defect visually and tactilely.
Under direct ultrasound guidance, the surgeon injects the prepared product at the level of the sphincter defect using a syringe.
The surgeon uses a one cc syringe to perform the injection while withdrawing the needle, minimizing the risk of ischemic consequences in the tissue.
The two-dimensional transanal ultrasound displays real-time imaging during the infiltration process, showing the needle as it enters the tissue.
The ultrasound image displays a hyper-echoic area at the nine to ten o'clock position, indicating the deposit of the injected product within the anal sphincter gap.
The surgeon continues the infiltration of the final product anteriorly, using ultrasound guidance to fill the visible gap at the sphincter defect.
The surgeon completes the injection along the entire length of the anal canal, aiming to fill the visible defect under ultrasound guidance.

The surgeon completes the injection of approximately thirty to thirty-two milliliters of the final product into the anal canal, resulting in a visible bulking effect that can be appreciated by palpation.
The surgeon checks the integrity of the mucosa of the anal canal and the vaginal wall, ensuring no damage or irregularities are visible at the end of the procedure.
The transanal ultrasound image displays the treated area, showing the effects of the procedure on the anal canal.
The ultrasound image displays hyperechoic deposits located at the level of the sphincter gap.
